intro_text:
Zuma Beach runs along the 30000 block of Pacific Coast Highway in Malibu, Los Angeles County, about 1.7 km from the town center. It is a Los Angeles County beach — operated by the county, with the gate hours, the eight paid lots, and the roughly 2,000 marked parking spaces that come with public ownership. That fact is the whole point of Zuma. Most of the Malibu coast is gated, private, and fought over; Zuma is the stretch where the public actually gets onto the sand, which is why on a hot weekend it fills with families from the San Fernando Valley and inland Los Angeles rather than the homeowners behind the PCH wall.

The numbers are large. The county lists 1.8 miles of beach frontage and 105 acres of property, with 14 lifeguard towers on the sand and one of only four Los Angeles County Lifeguard Section Headquarters at its center. The sand is genuinely wide — wide enough that even a packed summer day spreads out instead of stacking up — and it is backed not by houses but by the open lots and the highway, so the beach reads as one continuous bright plane rather than a string of private frontages.

The south end runs into Point Dume, the 100-foot headland that George Vancouver named in 1793 for Padre Francisco Dumetz. The point shelters the cove of Westward Beach next door and gives the whole bay its shape; from the bluff trail above it, gray whales are visible offshore on their December-to-April migration. Beyond Dume the coast turns private again almost immediately.

The water is colder than most Los Angeles beaches, and that is not folklore. Zuma faces open ocean with no protecting island or point on the swell window, so north winds drive upwelling that pulls deep, cold water to the surface; a few windy days can drop the temperature several degrees. Late-February lows sit around 13–15°C, and even the September peak only reaches the high teens. The same exposure that cools the water makes the surf — and the rip currents that run with it.

So Zuma is the working public face of a private coast: a big, cold, exposed, county-run beach that does the job Malibu's gated frontages refuse to. It has been a film set since 1968 and lent its name to a 1975 Neil Young record, but on an ordinary Tuesday it is mostly sand, lifeguard towers, and the Pacific doing exactly what an unprotected coast does.
spike_explainer:
Zuma's distinction is not a single wave or a shipwreck or a rock. It is **scale crossed with exposure, under public ownership** — and the specific, slightly grim consequence that follows from putting a very large, very open, very cold beach within driving range of millions of people who did not grow up swimming in it.

Start with the ownership, because it is the part most beaches in Malibu cannot claim. Malibu's coast is famous for being privatized to the waterline: gated frontages, contested public-access easements, and decades of legal fights to keep the strand in front of beach houses from being treated as the public tideland it legally is. Zuma is the exception that the public actually uses. It is a Los Angeles County beach at 30000 Pacific Coast Highway, operated by the county with eight numbered lots, roughly 2,000 parking spaces, and posted gate hours. The county figures are concrete: **1.8 miles of frontage, 105 acres, 14 lifeguard towers**, and one of only four Los Angeles County Lifeguard Section Headquarters sitting at the center of the sand. That headquarters placement is the tell — the county runs a major share of its Malibu-area rescue operation from this one beach, because this is the beach the crowds reach.

Now the exposure. Zuma faces the open Pacific. Unlike Santa Monica or the breaks tucked behind Point Dume, it has no island and no headland shadowing its swell window, so it takes ocean energy more or less head-on. Two things follow directly. The first is **cold water**. Open exposure plus north winds produces upwelling, the process that circulates deep, cold water to the surface; locals and the county both describe Zuma as running colder than other Los Angeles beaches, and a stretch of strong north wind can pull the surface temperature down several degrees in days. Late-February sea temperatures sit around 13–15°C and even the early-September peak only reaches the high teens — cold enough that surfers here wear 3/2 wetsuits in summer and 4/3s with boots in winter. The second consequence of exposure is the surf itself, and with the surf, the rips.

This is where the spike turns uncomfortable, and where honesty matters more than brochure copy. Zuma's wide, open, sandbar-bottomed beach generates rip currents — narrow, fast channels of water flowing back out to sea — and it generates them reliably enough that the **Los Angeles County Lifeguards have used Zuma as a teaching example of rip-current danger**. The stretch between Towers 8 and 9 is specifically noted as rip-prone. These are not abstract hazards. On bad-surf days the county's lifeguards run mass-rescue operations out of here; on one high-surf day a drowning at Zuma came amid 270 rescues across Los Angeles County beaches, with dozens of those at Zuma alone, and the beach has its own roll of fatal rip-current incidents over the years. The mechanism is exactly the one the warning signs describe: a swimmer wades out, gets pulled seaward in a channel faster than they can swim against, panics, and exhausts themselves fighting the current instead of swimming parallel to shore out of it.

Put the three together and you have the actual singular thing about Zuma. It is **the place where Malibu's private coast becomes public**, and the price of that openness is a beach that is genuinely demanding: big, cold, and rip-cut, fronting open ocean, absorbing crowds who often arrive from inland with weak ocean-swimming experience. That combination is why the lifeguard infrastructure here is so heavy — 14 towers and a section headquarters for one beach — and why Zuma, more than the sheltered, expert-only point breaks down the coast, is where Los Angeles County does the bulk of its teaching and its rescuing.

The culture grew on top of that geography rather than the other way around. Because Zuma is the accessible, photogenic, wide-open Malibu beach, it became a **film and television backdrop**: the eastern end of neighboring Westward Beach, in the shadow of Point Dume, is where the 1968 *Planet of the Apes* shot its Statue-of-Liberty finale, the matte-painted ruin set against the real headland. A 1978 NBC television movie was literally titled *Zuma Beach*. *Baywatch* used these sands. And in 1975 Neil Young and Crazy Horse, living and recording at producer David Briggs' house near the beach at Point Dume, named the resulting album **Zuma** after it. None of that is why the beach matters; it is what accrues to a beach that is, uniquely on this coast, both spectacular and open to everyone. The spike is the openness, and the cold, hard ocean that openness lets in.
honest_reckoning_note:
Zuma's openness is also its danger. The same unprotected exposure that makes it the public's beach makes it cold, surfy, and cut by rip currents — and the people it draws are often inland visitors without strong ocean-swimming experience. Los Angeles County Lifeguards use Zuma to illustrate rip-current danger, and flag the water between Towers 8 and 9 as particularly prone. The rescues are real and the drownings are real: on one high-surf day a fatal drowning here came amid 270 rescues across the county's beaches, dozens of them at Zuma. If you swim, swim in front of a staffed tower, and if a current pulls you out, swim parallel to the beach until you are free of it rather than straight back at the sand.

The water quality is the second honest caveat. In dry weather Zuma ranks among the cleaner Los Angeles County beaches, and Heal the Bay's monitoring usually grades it well. After rain it is a different beach: storm runoff drains to the surf, and following the 2018 Woolsey Fire — which burned much of the surrounding Santa Monica Mountains — Zuma and other normally clean Malibu beaches drew failing wet-weather grades as the scorched watershed flushed debris and bacteria to the coast. The standing public-health advice holds: stay out of the water for about 72 hours after significant rain.
reckoning_pullquote: The county lifeguards use Zuma to teach what a rip current does — because this is the open, cold, public beach where inland crowds meet an ocean they didn't grow up in.

key_facts.label: Beach frontage
key_facts.value: 1.8 miles of sand; 105 acres of property

key_facts.label: Lifeguard towers
key_facts.value: 14 towers on the sand, plus one of four L.A. County Lifeguard Section Headquarters at the beach center
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Zuma Beach; L.A. County Fire Lifeguard Division

key_facts.label: Parking
key_facts.value: Eight lots, roughly 2,000 spaces; gates open 6am-10pm

key_facts.label: Rip-current zone
key_facts.value: Water between Towers 8 and 9 noted as particularly rip-prone; county uses Zuma to illustrate rip-current danger
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Zuma Beach; L.A. County Lifeguards

key_facts.label: Sea temperature
key_facts.value: Colder than most L.A. beaches; ~13-15°C in late Feb, high teens at Sept peak (upwelling-driven)
key_facts.source: surf-forecast.com Zuma Beach sea-temp guide; L.A. County Beaches

key_facts.label: Point Dume
key_facts.value: 100-foot headland closing the south end; named 1793 by George Vancouver for Padre Francisco Dumetz
key_facts.source: Wikipedia: Zuma Beach; Wikipedia: Point Dume

margin_notes.text: Zuma is the public-access exception on a coast built to keep the public out. Most Malibu frontage is gated and litigated to the waterline; this county beach is where Valley and inland-L.A. families actually get onto the sand.

Fourteen lifeguard towers and a full Section Headquarters for one beach is unusual. The heavy infrastructure exists because the open exposure plus inland crowds produces a steady volume of rip-current rescues.
Point Dume's bluff trail is a public gray-whale lookout from December to April, when the migration passes close offshore. The headland also shelters the Westward Beach cove immediately south.

Zuma runs cold because it faces open ocean with no island or point shadowing its swell window. North winds drive upwelling, pulling deep cold water up; a few windy days can drop the surface several degrees.
The eastern end of neighboring Westward Beach, below Point Dume, is where Planet of the Apes (1968) shot its Statue-of-Liberty finale — a matte painting blended into the real rock of the headland.
Neil Young and Crazy Horse recorded the 1975 album Zuma while living at producer David Briggs' house near the beach at Point Dume, and named the record after it.

day_in_time.dawn: Before the gates and the crowd, Zuma is at its emptiest and its surf at its cleanest, with the offshore usually lightest at first light. The marine layer often sits grey over the water into mid-morning. The towers are still shuttered; a few surfers in full wetsuits are out at the open peaks, and the cold of the upwelled water is most obvious now.
day_in_time.midday: By late morning the eight lots are filling and the wide sand spreads the crowd out rather than stacking it. Lifeguards have the 14 towers staffed and the flags up; this is when the rip-current rescues happen, especially on a building summer swell between Towers 8 and 9. Volleyball nets are in use, the seasonal food stands are open, and Pacific Coast Highway backs up at the lot entrances.
day_in_time.golden: Late afternoon throws long light down the open beach, with Point Dume's headland turning to silhouette at the south end. Because the coast faces roughly south-southwest here, the sun drops toward the ocean off Dume. The water has usually warmed a little through the day; the crowd thins as families pack out, leaving the wide sand to walkers and the evening surf session.
day_in_time.night: After the 10pm gate close the lots empty and the beach is dark and unlit except by the highway. There are no lifeguards after daylight, and the surf and rips that the towers watch all day run unwatched — the county's standing reason not to swim Zuma at night. On a clear night the cold open Pacific is just sound and a black horizon under Point Dume.

food_drink.name: Seasonal beach concession stands
food_drink.description: Zuma's food is the county-beach concession setup — snack and drink stands on the sand that open seasonally and on busy days, not a restaurant row. Bring a cooler; this is a pack-in beach more than a dine-out one.
food_drink.where: On the sand at Zuma Beach, seasonal, near the main lots

food_drink.name: Fish tacos
food_drink.description: The Southern California beach baseline: grilled or battered white fish, cabbage, crema, and lime on a corn tortilla. The Malibu and PCH taco counters within a short drive are where Zuma-goers eat after a session with sand still on their feet.
food_drink.where: Taco shops and counters along Pacific Coast Highway in Malibu

food_drink.name: Malibu seafood on PCH
food_drink.description: Casual fried-and-grilled seafood served counter-style is the regional Malibu-coast staple — fish and chips, steamed shellfish, ceviche eaten at outdoor tables across the highway from the water. Located off the beach itself, a short drive south toward central Malibu.
food_drink.where: Pacific Coast Highway, Malibu (south of Zuma toward the pier)

timeline.title: Point Dume named by George Vancouver
timeline.description: British explorer George Vancouver named the headland at Zuma's south end after Padre Francisco Dumetz of Mission San Buenaventura; the spelling was later anglicized to Dume. The point defines the bay Zuma sits in.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Point Dume; Wikipedia: Zuma Beach

timeline.title: "Point Zuma or Duma" on the plat map
timeline.description: An 1870 plat map of the area marked the location as "Point Zuma or Duma" — an early documented use of the Zuma name, which is generally traced to a Chumash place name for the area.

timeline.title: Planet of the Apes films its finale here
timeline.description: The Statue-of-Liberty ending of Planet of the Apes (1968) was shot at the eastern end of neighboring Westward Beach, below Point Dume — the ruined statue a matte painting blended into the real rock of the headland.
timeline.source: movie-locations.com; Wikipedia: Planet of the Apes (1968 film)

timeline.title: Neil Young's album "Zuma" released
timeline.description: Neil Young and Crazy Horse, living and recording at producer David Briggs' house near the beach at Point Dume, named their November 1975 album Zuma after it. It was the first Young/Crazy Horse co-credit in six years and the first with Frank Sampedro on guitar.

timeline.title: NBC TV movie "Zuma Beach" airs
timeline.description: Zuma Beach, a TV movie written by John Carpenter and William Schwartz, directed by Lee H. Katzin and starring Suzanne Somers, aired on NBC on September 27, 1978. Its cast included future stars Rosanna Arquette, Timothy Hutton, and Michael Biehn.

timeline.title: Baywatch begins filming on the coast
timeline.description: The lifeguard drama Baywatch, which ran from 1989 to 2001, used Los Angeles County beaches including Zuma as filming locations — fittingly, given Zuma is a real L.A. County Lifeguard Section Headquarters.

timeline.title: Used to illustrate rip-current danger
timeline.description: By 2007 the Los Angeles County lifeguard department was using Zuma to illustrate rip-current hazards, with the water between Towers 8 and 9 flagged as particularly prone. The beach's open exposure makes it a standing teaching case for rip safety.
timeline.source: Wikipedia: Zuma Beach; L.A. County Lifeguards

timeline.title: Woolsey Fire fouls post-rain water quality
timeline.description: The 2018 Woolsey Fire burned much of the surrounding Santa Monica Mountains watershed. In the rains that followed, normally clean Zuma and other Malibu beaches drew failing wet-weather grades from Heal the Bay as the scorched watershed flushed debris and bacteria to the surf.
timeline.source: Heal the Bay Beach Report Card; Messenger Mountain News

timeline.title: Malibu triathlon rebranded as the Zuma Beach Triathlon
timeline.description: The long-running Malibu Nautica Triathlon, a benefit for Children's Hospital Los Angeles staged on this coast, was rebranded as the Zuma Beach Triathlon in 2023.

zones.name: Central Zuma / Section Headquarters
zones.character: The heart of the beach, fronting the main lots, with the Los Angeles County Lifeguard Section Headquarters and the densest tower coverage. The widest, busiest sand and the easiest access straight off the parking lots.
zones.best_for: Families, swimmers near a staffed tower, volleyball, the simplest beach day
zones.notes: Busiest and most-staffed stretch; the natural place to set up if you want lifeguards close.

zones.name: Towers 8-9 (north-central)
zones.character: The stretch the county flags as particularly rip-prone. Wide open sand and exposed surf; reads calm but produces fast seaward channels on a building swell.
zones.best_for: Experienced ocean swimmers and surfers who understand rips
zones.notes: If you swim here, stay in front of a staffed tower and know how to escape a rip — swim parallel to shore, not against it.

zones.name: South end toward Point Dume / Westward
zones.character: Where Zuma narrows toward the Point Dume headland and the adjacent Westward Beach cove. Rockier, more sheltered by the point, and the launch for the Dume bluff trail and its whale-watching overlook.
zones.best_for: Walkers, whale-watching (Dec-Apr) from the bluff, quieter sand away from the main lots
zones.notes: Westward Beach has its own access and lot; the Planet of the Apes finale was shot at its eastern end.

landmarks.name: Point Dume
landmarks.description: The roughly 100-foot coastal headland closing Zuma's south end, named in 1793 by George Vancouver for Padre Francisco Dumetz. It forms the northern end of Santa Monica Bay, shelters the Westward Beach cove, and carries a state natural preserve with a bluff-top whale-watching overlook used during the December-to-April gray whale migration.

landmarks.name: Zuma Beach Lifeguard Section Headquarters
landmarks.description: One of only four Los Angeles County Lifeguard Section Headquarters, sited at the center of Zuma Beach. Its placement here — rather than at a sheltered beach — reflects Zuma's role as the open, crowded, rip-prone beach where the county concentrates rescue operations.

cultural_refs.title: Zuma
cultural_refs.creator: Neil Young and Crazy Horse
cultural_refs.description: Neil Young's November 1975 album, named after Zuma Beach. Young, Crazy Horse, and producer David Briggs lived and recorded near the beach at Point Dume; it was the first Young/Crazy Horse co-credit in six years and includes "Cortez the Killer."

cultural_refs.title: Planet of the Apes
cultural_refs.creator: Franklin J. Schaffner (director)
cultural_refs.description: The film's Statue-of-Liberty finale was shot at the eastern end of Westward Beach, below Point Dume at Zuma's south end. The ruined statue was a matte painting blended into the real rock of the headland.

cultural_refs.title: Zuma Beach
cultural_refs.creator: Lee H. Katzin (director); written by John Carpenter and William Schwartz
cultural_refs.description: An NBC television movie named for the beach, aired September 27, 1978, starring Suzanne Somers as a rock singer who falls in among teenage beachgoers. The cast included future stars Rosanna Arquette, Timothy Hutton, and Michael Biehn.

cultural_refs.title: Baywatch
cultural_refs.description: The lifeguard drama (1989-2001) filmed on Los Angeles County beaches including Zuma — apt, since Zuma is a genuine L.A. County Lifeguard Section Headquarters rather than a set version of one.

recurring_events.name: Zuma Beach Triathlon (formerly Malibu Nautica Triathlon)
recurring_events.description: A long-running triathlon on this coast staged as a benefit for Children's Hospital Los Angeles, rebranded from the Malibu Nautica Triathlon to the Zuma Beach Triathlon in 2023.

recurring_events.name: Gray whale migration watch
recurring_events.description: Not an organized event but a reliable seasonal draw: California gray whales pass close offshore on their migration and are watched from the Point Dume bluff overlook at Zuma's south end.

things_to_know.label: Rip currents and lifeguard towers
things_to_know.note: Zuma is open-ocean beach with reliable rip currents; the county specifically flags the water between Towers 8 and 9. Swim in front of a staffed tower during daylight, and if caught in a rip, swim parallel to the beach until you are out of it rather than straight back to shore.

things_to_know.label: Cold water, bring a wetsuit
things_to_know.note: Upwelling keeps Zuma colder than most L.A. beaches — roughly 13-15°C in late winter, high teens at the early-September peak. Surfers wear a 3/2 wetsuit in summer and a 4/3 with boots in winter; even swimmers feel the chill.

things_to_know.label: Avoid the water for ~72 hours after rain
things_to_know.note: Zuma is usually clean in dry weather but storm runoff fouls it, and the post-2018-Woolsey-Fire watershed made wet-weather grades worse. Stay out of the surf for about three days after significant rain.

things_to_know.label: Paid lots, early fill, gate hours
things_to_know.note: Eight county lots with roughly 2,000 spaces, gated 6am-10pm. They fill on summer weekends; arrive before mid-morning. There are no lifeguards and no swimming once it's dark.

access_note: Zuma is at 30000 Pacific Coast Highway in Malibu, roughly an hour by car from Los Angeles International Airport (LAX) without traffic, north on I-405 then west on PCH (CA-1). There is no rail to the beach. Drive in and use the eight paid county lots, or take Metro Bus Line 534, which runs along Pacific Coast Highway with a stop at PCH and Morning View near the beach. Avoid weekday rush hours and summer weekend mid-mornings, when both PCH and the lots back up.
